title: Add make depend, no build if there are no changes
body: Generate depend files for make, using a `go list` script.

comments.body: @afbjorklund what exactily does the depend: directive do in a Makefile?  Does this cause `all:` to check if anything listed under `depend:` has changed?

@rhatdan : it was just a phony target (similar to `all`) to generate all the depend files... Normally it is never needed, since make will automatically generate the files that it includes if they are missing.

Unfortunately Go is not yet able to generate dependencies while it is compiling objects, unlike modern C compilers... So that means that we still need to run `go` twice: once to "list" and once to "build".

@afbjorklund, thanks for the PR! I don't think we need this optimization as `go-build` doesn't recompile when there are no changes. However, it does link the objects which is fairly cheap in comparison to compiler optimizations.

What I find very important is sensitivity to changes to the environment, which the proposed script in this PR is not, see below:

```bash
[valentin@nebuchadnezzar buildah]$ TAGS="123" make buildah
make: 'buildah' is up to date.
```

IMHO we should stick with the go tooling. You can try an incremental compilation by first cleaning the cache `rm -rf ~/.cache/go-build` and then perform two compilations. On my machine the time drops from around 14 seconds to around 2.5 seconds.
Yeah, I suppose it makes for a bigger difference with big projects like minikube.

For me it takes 4.5 seconds to build (with cache). Running make take 0.8 seconds*.
As a comparison it takes 0.5 seconds to list the depends, and 30 seconds without cache.

\* The main reason why make is so "slow", is because of RCS. Running `make -r` is 10x.
https://www.gnu.org/software/make/manual/html_node/Catalogue-of-Rules.html
